frames.name: Corporate resistance to military overreach
frames.description: Frames the story as Anthropic standing up against Pentagon demands to remove ethical restrictions on AI use, particularly around lethal autonomous weapons and mass surveillance
frames.emphasis: Anthropic's objections to allowing AI for 'fully control lethal autonomous weapons' and 'mass surveillance on US citizens'

frames.name: Presidential authority vs. tech company obstruction
frames.description: Presents the conflict as Trump asserting federal authority against a 'leftwing' tech company trying to dictate military policy
frames.emphasis: Trump's characterization of Anthropic as 'Leftwing nut jobs' making a 'DISASTROUS MISTAKE trying to STRONG-ARM the Department of War'

frames.name: Silicon Valley's evolving relationship with defense
frames.description: Contextualizes the dispute within the broader trend of tech companies moving from avoiding defense work to embracing it as military contractors
frames.emphasis: The shift from 'largely avoiding defense work to increasingly embracing it and eventually becoming full-blown military contractors'

outlierPerspectives: Worker solidarity across AI companies - mention of 'several hundred workers from OpenAI and Google' signing an open letter supporting Anthropic and criticizing their own companies
Anthropic's unique position as the only AI company working with classified systems despite being first to partner with military
missingContext: Specific details about what 'all lawful use' would permit that current restrictions prevent
Pentagon's official response or justification for the policy change demands
Details about the original July deal terms and what restrictions were initially agreed upon
Anthropic's official statement or response to the ban announcement
Potential impact on ongoing military operations or intelligence work
Legal framework governing AI use in military applications
Comparison with how other AI companies (Google, OpenAI, xAI) are handling similar Pentagon demands
